narrative: I'm sorry to share this news, but the law school's admission model predicted a 76% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application was rejected—they use this to prioritize students likely to succeed. Your profile includes a Year 1 school decile of 1, a Year 3 decile of 1, middle family income, an LSAT score of 28, an undergraduate GPA of 2.5, being female, Black, and planning part-time work. Compared to typical students, your LSAT is well below the average of about 37, your undergrad GPA is lower than the usual 3.2, and your early and later school deciles are in the bottom ranks versus mid-level averages around 5-6. The model's decision was driven most strongly by your LSAT score, which had the biggest influence and pushed the prediction toward expecting bar failure since it's so much lower than what succeeds for others. Next in influence came your Year 3 decile rank, also pointing strongly toward failure due to its low standing relative to peers. Your Year 1 decile rank followed closely, reinforcing that early struggle with the same negative pull. Family income in the middle bracket had a moderate role, nudging toward failure compared to higher typical levels, while your undergrad GPA contributed similarly by being below average. Your racial background as Black had some influence toward the failure prediction, given its rarity in the successful dataset, and part-time work plans added a smaller push in that direction too. The one factor that slightly helped, pulling gently toward passing, was your gender as female, which is a bit less common but favorable in the data. Overall, these ranked from LSAT as top influencer down to gender as least, painting a picture of academic metrics lagging behind successful applicants. To improve your odds next time, focus on what you can change: retake the LSAT aiming for 35+, boost your GPA through post-bac courses if possible, and consider full-time enrollment to align with the norm. Building stronger academic deciles via targeted study or community college refreshers could help too, while family income and background are harder to shift but don't define your potential. With dedication, you can retune these key areas and reapply stronger. Keep pushing—you've got this.
